author: @stevecummins324
text:
I have an idea for an arrangement for a CVT module that, after a fashion, can be implemented with gears. There's an old 1930 "art" film Mechanical Principles - which is well worth watching by those interested in unusual and interesting mechanisms.  Within it is shown a few non circular gears in operation. The ratio through a non-circular gear pair varies as they rotate.

if a such a gear was driven by an oscillating rotation(of less than one complete rotation) the gear ratio will vary but only within a limited range. A differential gearbox could be used to adjust the phasing of the oscillation (adding or subtracting a temporally fixed  angle from the oscillating rotation) and thus change the range over which the non-circular gear is transmitting torque.

author: @steveellis8347
text: Have you considered using a centrifugal clutch to regulate?  Bringing a smaller/secondary generator online would regulate speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: how would that work mate? I have never considered it

replies.author: @steveellis8347
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  You would lengthen the primary generators shaft enough so you could mount the input side of the Centrifugal Clutch (CC)    The output side of the CC typically has a pully or sprocket which could be connected to the shaft of the second generator.  So when the first generators speed is significant enough to engage the CC it would spin up the second generator which would utilize the extra energy.  It would be a balancing act, as the velocity would have to be enough to start the second generator without bogging the whole thing down.  Then if wind died down the CC would disengage.  I've been thinking of this as a better solution to the braking system some wind turbines use when the wind is too strong

author: @SoundzAlive1
text: Hi Rob, I am wondering if you will use a Vespa (and others) style CVT gearbox sometime? It is two discs with a belt in between going to another set of discs where the gap varies from narrow near the centre to wide at the outside edge. This allows gearing as you change the gap the belt moves in or out giving different gear ratios. Brilliant in that making one gear larger causes the other to reduce as the length of the belt does not change. So one gear goes from start to top ratio no need to change gears ! André in Sydney

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I believe they are actually cones mate not gears

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: French mopeds use that approach too, except there is only one pair of discs, on the engine side, while on the wheel side there is a simple pulley, and the whole engine swings back to compensate for the change in length as the discs squeeze the belt outwards. It seems a bit complicated but it works. I have no idea what controls the spacing of the discs but it's proportional to the load. I like the idea of using two pairs of discs better though as the distance between the input and output axle doesn't change, but again, wonder what controls the spacing.

author: @bencallender9940
text: The friction disk drive arrangement on some snowblower wheel drives is essentially a cvt. Of course, they usually have slots for the selector to lock the mechanism into preset ratios, but it provides a constant torque output that the scotch yoke lacks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is and like anything there are 100 ways to skin a cat - both have good points and bad points - for example a friction cvt is cheap but prone to slip and has terrible efficiency - and i mean terrible lol - but it is a well used solution in some circumstances where its good points outweigh it's bad points - like in lawn mowers

author: @deslomeslager
text: Not having access to the real Skinner machine makes it difficult to draw conclusions on his specific device. It may work like a rocket which is going through the universe and needs a (speed) boost. It may use gravity to get this. Rockets used this techniques before, it is a sling shot, and the rocket approaches a big mass and leaves the orbit with more speed. Why wouldn't Skinner have found a way to do this? I also have the impression this same slingshot technique can be sued to harness energy via magnets. (I do not have any answers though, I am only saying this out loud).

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i guess it really depends on how you see things mate - I see it as a simple mechanical device of terrestrial origin operating on planet earth and therefore described by simple explanations - other people have different views on it - you can see complexity in it if you wish - i just don't see that as helpful - but it is only my opinion

author: @zerker2000
text:
"It's argued that all machines are really just built-up simple machines, and the same principle - the principle of levers - operates on all machines"
I'm not sure I exactly buy this for, say, a serpentine coil generator hooked up to a light bulb
except in some metaphorical sense

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Why not? What - for you - is special about a serpentine coil?

replies.author: @zerker2000
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringRephrasing slightly: say you have one of those solar panel landscape lights, that charge in the day, and glow at night. What, in this mechanism with no moving parts, is akin to a lever? The battery is perhaps akin to a pulley, translating force across time instead of direction.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's not a mechanism  - a mechanism by definition has moving parts - The German scientist Franz Reuleaux defines machine as "a combination of resistant bodies so arranged that by their means the mechanical forces of nature can be compelled to do work accompanied by certain determinate motion". In this context, his use of machine is generally interpreted to mean mechanism.

replies.author: @zerker2000
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringah, something like a computer speaker being "not a machine" because the forces are not resistant/mechanical, resolves this nicely fair enough!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate I am not trying to be silly here but a speaker is a machine - it's actually just a lever - a second class lever - the force acting on it is the electromagnetic repulsion of the coil magnet arrangement the fulcrum is at the end and the load is the air it is moving

author: @MediumPointBallPoint
text:
First! And I'm glad you address this. Often in your videos you get excited about voltage, but more important is total energy ( volts times current times time ). Your small demos often produce only small amounts of energy ( work ), and I have often thought you should spend more time explaining that.
To be sure, there are only limited amounts of power available, and often it is a question of size and scale.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Great point! - unfortunately this video will not get many views - actually, I often take time to explain principles and I am often disappointed on how many views they get
